data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a technological paradigm shift where the democratization of iron-working rendered the bronze-based monopoly of the palaces obsolete.", "mechanism": "The sudden availability of cheap iron tools and weapons allowed marginalized groups to bypass the palace-controlled tin/copper supply chain, eroding the economic basis of elite power and enabling successful asymmetrical warfare against bronze-equipped charioteers.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Metallurgical analysis showing iron artifacts were rare, ritualistic, or inferior to bronze in the region until after 1100 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical view challenging the standard chronology that places widespread iron use post-collapse; posits technology as the driver rather than the result."}
